input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a 1/10-scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology, featuring lower and upper chassis layers along with autonomy elements. It operates within the automotive domain for Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) applications, where resource-constrained electronic control units (ECUs) necessitate offloading computation to a digital twin for real-time monitoring, data reporting, and remote management capabilities.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides three services: 1) Data Monitoring via a user interface (UI) that displays real-time vehicle state data, projected driving paths, and detected object tracks, with capabilities to save and generate reports; 2) Computation Offloading where the ACC system's lead vehicle detection, speed computation based on distance, and actuation commands are processed externally (e.g., cloud) to alleviate on-board hardware constraints; 3) Remote Management enabling operators to enable/disable ACC, set desired speeds, and send emergency braking commands remotely. These services support operators for monitoring and control and developers for troubleshooting using data collection.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation utilizes NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 SDK for software development, Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) as middleware for communication between components, Python 3.8 and C++ for programming, a SQL database for structured data storage and reporting, and Wi-Fi network infrastructure for low-latency communication between the physical twin and the cloud-based DT services.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The system requires 100 ms clock synchronization accuracy for computation offloading between devices, as mandated by safety-critical vehicular application standards, though hardware limitations prevent stricter synchronization than this threshold.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The system employs a decentralized architecture with three distinct entities (User, Digital Twin, Physical Twin) deployed on separate hardware components. The User Entity hosts 3D Visualization and Control Panel ROS2 nodes; the Digital Twin Entity manages data processing and control mechanisms; the Physical Twin Entity handles sensor data pre-processing, actuation, and automotive functions such as Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking, deviating from ISO 23247's Operational Management Entity by incorporating broader system resources.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin architecture follows ISO 23247 RA with entity-based structuring, mapping software components to Functional Entities implemented as ROS2 packages. Each entity (User, Digital Twin, Physical Twin) is deployed on separate hardware, with the Physical Twin Entity extending beyond ISO 23247's OME to host general automotive functionality like Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking. The User Interface FE is split into 3D Visualization and Control Panel components, while Data Storage lacks a direct FE alignment.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The system employs geometric models for object representation, behavioral models using DBSCAN clustering of raw LiDAR point clouds, and analytical Kalman filters for trajectory tracking. Sensor data is gathered into a SQL database for persistent storage and real-time access.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Physical sensor data including LiDAR point clouds, vehicle state, motor actuation, and steering servo inputs are collected via Data Collecting FE. Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking processes raw LiDAR data, and Data Translation serializes data, aligns message types, and converts units. Communication between software components uses ROS2, while inter-entity communication uses UDP over Wi-Fi. Data types transmitted are raw point cloud, vehicle state, object tracks, and projected driving path.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands such as emergency braking and desired speed adjustments are generated by the Controlling FE and sent to the Actuation component, which converts desired speed into motor voltage commands. The Control Panel User Interface FE enables remote management of ACC, with data translation ensuring proper command formatting for physical actuation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Internal software components communicate via ROS2, while inter-entity communication uses Wi-Fi. Requirement R7 mandates a maximum network latency of 100 ms. No details on bandwidth, security protocols, or redundancy mechanisms are specified in the document.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering and development process includes DTS Requirements (Section IV.A), Architecture (Section IV.B), and Implementation of Services (Section IV.C), detailing system requirements specification, structural design, and service deployment methodologies.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The system provides Data Monitoring and Remote Management services with Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) logic for decision support, utilizing 3D Visualization interfaces for real-time monitoring and control of the Observable Manufacturing Element (OME), where Functional Entities transform data into human-interpretable formats for actionable insights.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: ISO 23247:2021 standard defines the Digital Twin framework for manufacturing, with four parts: Overview and general principles, Reference architecture (Part 2), Digital representation of physical manufacturing elements, and Information exchange. Part 2 specifies the reference architecture entities including Observable Manufacturing Element (OME), Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity. Functional Entities handle specific tasks like data transformation, with OPC Unified Architecture cited as an implementation example for interoperability.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_1_judge.reasoning: The description accurately captures the physical twin's specifications and purpose. It correctly identifies the scale ('1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle'), the construction method ('retrofitted with autonomy components'), the chassis structure ('lower level chassis', 'upper level chassis'), the application ('Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)'), and the motivation for the digital twin ('resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU)').

The extraction perfectly aligns with Section III.A of the text. It correctly details the three services: Data Monitoring ('view, save, and generate reports', 'display real-time vehicle state data'), Computation Offloading ('alleviate the computational burden', 'determines the lead vehicle, then computes a desired speed'), and Remote Management ('enabling/disabling ACC', 'setting the desired speed', 'sending an emergency braking command'). It also correctly identifies the user groups ('operators and developers').

The extraction correctly lists the specific software and hardware tools mentioned in the document: NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 SDK, ROS2 middleware, Python 3.8, C++, a SQL database, and Wi-Fi infrastructure. The source explicitly states: 'We use NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 as the Software Development Kit (SDK)', 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) as the middleware', 'Application modules are implemented in Python 3.8 ... C++', and in Table I, 'Data Storage Gathers object tracks ... into a SQL database'. The mention of Wi-Fi is supported by the final sentence of the provided text: 'inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extracted characteristic accurately captures the temporal requirements mentioned in the document. It correctly cites the '100 ms clock synchronization accuracy' and explains that this is due to hardware limitations despite stricter standards for safety-critical applications. It also correctly links this to 'computation offloading' as the reason for synchronization needs. This is strongly supported by the text: 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms... To enable computation offloading... We require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.' and 'The DTS shall maintain network latency of at most 100 ms from the vehicle to the DT Entity.'

The description accurately reflects the decentralized architecture described in the text, identifying the three distinct entities (User, Digital Twin, Physical Twin) deployed on separate hardware. It correctly specifies the ROS2 nodes within the User Entity ('3D Visualization' and 'Control Panel') and accurately describes the responsibilities of the Digital Twin and Physical Twin Entities. It also correctly notes the deviation from ISO 23247 regarding the Physical Twin Entity hosting general automotive functions like 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking'. This is supported by quotes: 'The existence of this entity marks a departure from the ISO 23247 RA... Physical Twin Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system... and can host general automotive functionality, such as Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking.'

The description correctly summarizes the system orchestration based on ISO 23247 RA. It accurately states that software components are mapped to Functional Entities implemented as ROS2 packages and deployed on separate hardware. It correctly details the decomposition of the User Interface FE into 3D Visualization and Control Panel, and correctly identifies that Data Storage lacks a direct FE alignment. It also accurately highlights the deviation of the Physical Twin Entity from the standard OME. Evidence includes: 'User Interface FE is instantiated in the 3D Visualization and Control Panel components', 'Physical Twin Entity... can host general automotive functionality', and 'Data Storage did not have a naturally aligned FE.'

The characteristic states 'Not in Document'. Upon reviewing the source text, the document focuses on the internal architecture of the Digital Twin system (ISO 23247 mapping, ROS2 implementation, hardware deployment) and the ACC case study. It does not mention integration with external systems, enterprise systems, other Digital Twins, or specific interoperability standards beyond the internal ROS2/Wi-Fi setup. Therefore, the assessment that this characteristic is not present in the document is accurate.

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

States 'Not in Document', which is correct. Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. Raw point cloud data from the LiDAR sensor must be processed by the _Point Cloud_ _Clustering & Tracking_ SW component, which turns the raw point cloud, represented as individual points on a 2D plane,

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies specific data types (LiDAR point clouds, vehicle state), software components (Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking, Data Translation), and the internal use of ROS2. The text supports the ROS2 communication: 'Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network', and the Data Translation role: 'This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions'. However, the score is not a 5 because the extraction includes the unsupported claim 'UDP' (the source text cuts off at 'using U') and the phrase 'motor actuation' being collected is technically imprecise (actuation is an output). It also misses the specific latency constraint of '100 ms' found in the text.

The extraction correctly describes the control flow, identifying the Controlling FE, specific commands (emergency braking, desired speed), and the Actuation component's role in converting speed to motor voltage. It also correctly links the Control Panel UI to remote management. This is strongly supported by the text. Supported by quotes: 'ACC Determines lead vehicle and computes following speed. Controlling FE', 'Actuation Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands. Actuation FE', 'Control Panel Interface for the user to send remote management commands.'.

The extraction accurately reports the internal (ROS2) and external (Wi-Fi) communication protocols, as well as the specific latency requirement from the text. It correctly notes the absence of details regarding bandwidth and security. Supported by quotes: 'Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network', 'inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network', 'R7. The DTS shall maintain network latency of at most 100 ms'.

The extraction correctly states 'Not in Document'. While the text mentions general deployment facts such as entities being 'deployed on a different hardware component' and storage on a 'device external to the vehicle', it lacks the specific details required by the characteristic definition: 'hosting platforms (cloud/edge/on-premise)', 'computational and storage requirements', or 'scalability mechanisms'. Therefore, the specific information required for this characteristic is absent.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task

block_5_judge.reasoning: The characteristic is marked as 'Not in Document', and the provided text does not contain a description of the Digital Twin's lifecycle phases, transition management between phases, or lifecycle governance approaches. While the text mentions the 'design phase' and 'operation phase' of automotive systems in a general context (Section II.B), it does not describe the lifecycle of the Digital Twin itself as requested by the characteristic definition. Therefore, the extraction is correct.

The extracted characteristic accurately summarizes the engineering process as including DTS Requirements, Architecture, and Implementation of Services, which is supported by the text: 'We now provide a detailed exposition of the case study, starting from the definition of DTS requirements (Sec. IV-A). We then elaborate on the architectural design (Sec. IV-B) and the implementation of DTS services (Sec. IV.C).' However, the text does not cover quality assurance, testing, validation, version control, or evolution management, so the extraction lacks depth on those aspects.

The extraction correctly describes decision support capabilities, including Data Monitoring and Remote Management services, ACC logic, and 3D Visualization interfaces, as supported by the text: 'To implement the ACC-DT, we develop the following three services through a DT. 1) Data Monitoring... 2) Computation Offloading... 3) Remote Management...' and 'The UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC remotely.' It also mentions Functional Entities transforming data into human-interpretable formats, aligning with the text. However, confidence measures for recommendations are not discussed.

The description is highly specific and detailed, accurately summarizing the ISO 23247 standard's structure and content from Section II.A. It correctly lists the four parts of the standard, the specific reference architecture entities (OME, Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity), and mentions 'OPC Unified Architecture' as an implementation example for interoperability.
